FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study baseline 2016-2018 through 2-year follow-up (ages 9-10 years at baseline), N=8,085 participants

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying measured as single item 'Have you ever been cyberbullied...on social media like Instagram or Snapchat?' combined with traditional bullying victimization (Peer Experiences Questionnaire) into one dichotomized 'any bullying victimization' variable. No separately extractable smartphone/SM-specific coefficient. Per E6, cyberbullying alone without separately-measured phone/SM variable = NOT_MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression models with two main effects testing ACEs and bullying victimization associations with internalizing and externalizing outcomes; interaction term examined. Table 2 reports coefficients and p-values for main and interaction effects.

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 2 reports β coefficients with 95% CI and p-values. Example: ACEs main effect B=1.92 [1.27-2.58], p<.001 for internalizing; bullying victimization B=1.28 [0.72-1.84], p<.001 for internalizing.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis using baseline to 2-year follow-up data with linear regression and interaction testing

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Youth and Adolescence 2024; DOI: 10.1007/s10964-023-01907-2

FT_EC7_reason: FT_IC2 NOT_MET. EC7 does not apply when inclusion criteria fail.
notes: C1 and C2 disagreed on FT_IC2. C1 correctly identified cyberbullying as NOT separately measured from traditional bullying and applied E6. C2 incorrectly treated the cyberbullying single item as a qualifying phone/SM exposure. The cyberbullying item is embedded within a broader bullying measure with no separate coefficient reported for SM-only harassment. Per prompt E6, cyberbullying alone with no separately-measured phone/SM variable = NOT_MET.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study 3.0 release, baseline 2016-2018, COVID RRR Survey 1 (May 2020), N=6,749 adolescents aged 12-13

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as aggregate composite across 7 modalities: multiple-player gaming, single-player gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, browsing internet, and watching/streaming videos/television (excluding schoolwork). Total screen time calculated by summing all seven modalities with no separately extractable smartphone or social media coefficient. Generic total screen time composite = NOT_MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression analyses with ACE score (0-9 continuous) predicting screen time and physical activity outcomes; adjusted models with multiple covariates

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 2 reports adjusted B = 0.44 (95% CI 0.26, 0.62), p < 0.001 for ACEs predicting hours per day screen time; youth with ≥4 ACEs averaged 2.3 more hours screen time per day vs 0 ACEs

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis - cross-sectional analysis from May 2020 COVID RRR Survey data using linear regression

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Academic Pediatrics 2022;22:1294-1299; DOI: 10.1016/j.acap.2022.07.007

FT_EC7_reason: FT_IC2 NOT_MET. EC7 does not apply when inclusion criteria fail.
notes: C1 and C2 disagreed on FT_IC2. C1 correctly identified screen time as generic composite without separately extractable smartphone/SM coefficient. C2 incorrectly treated the multi-device composite as qualifying. The Methods section explicitly states 'Total screen time was calculated by summing average screen time of these seven modalities' with no breakdown reported separately. Per criteria, generic total screen time composite = NOT_MET.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD study data (5.0 release) with nearly 10,000 children aged 9-10 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured using questionnaire where 'participants reported the amount of time they spent on digital devices, including television, videos, video games, texting, and video chat.' ABCD study showed screen use for weekdays and weekends separately; a daily average was calculated across all modalities combined. No separately extractable smartphone or social media coefficient. Generic total screen time composite = NOT_MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Criterion contingent on FT_IC2 MET; FT_IC2 fails

FT_IC4.evidence: Criterion contingent on FT_IC2 MET; FT_IC2 fails

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using linear mixed-effects models and mediation analysis with ABCD data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Acta Psychologica 2026, 265, 106742

FT_EC7_reason: FT_IC2 NOT_MET. EC7 does not apply when inclusion criteria fail.
notes: C1 and C2 disagreed on FT_IC2. C1 correctly identified screen time as generic composite including television, videos, video games, texting, and video chat combined without separate extraction. C2 incorrectly treated this as qualifying by labeling it 'specific smartphone/digital media variable.' Methods section shows daily average calculated by formula: (5/7 × weekday screen time) + (2/7 × weekend screen time) across all modalities. No phone/SM-specific breakdown reported. Generic total screen time = NOT_MET.
